original_abstract:
This abstract examines the structural transformation and operational dynamics of the Soviet war economy during the Great Patriotic War (1941–1945), based on the seminal analysis by N.A. Voznesensky. It outlines the strategic shift of the nation’s productive forces from western territories to the Urals, Siberia, and Central Asia—a massive logistical feat that preserved the USSR's industrial core despite catastrophic initial territorial losses. The text details the systemic transition from a civilian to a military-oriented socialist economy, characterized by the centralized mobilization of labor, rigorous state planning, and the rapid expansion of heavy industry and armaments production.

Key themes include the comparative advantages of a planned economy over the Russian Empire’s performance in World War I, the critical role of women and youth in filling labor shortages, and the "expanded reproduction" of industry in eastern regions. The study also provides a harrowing account of the material devastation in occupied zones and the subsequent heroic efforts to reconstruct liberated areas while the conflict was still ongoing. Ultimately, the abstract highlights how the pre-war industrial base, created during the Five-Year Plans, served as the essential foundation for achieving qualitative and quantitative superiority in military technology, leading to the eventual economic and military defeat of Nazi Germany.
humanized_abstract:
This abstract deals with the Soviet war economy’s structural transformation and the functioning of the Soviet war economy during the Great Patriotic War period (1941–1945) based on the work of N.A. Voznesensky. The author focuses on the transfer of the nation’s productive forces to the Urals, Siberia and Central Asia from the Western regions that had been occupied by the enemy in the first period of the war. This was a gigantic and highly organized operation that enabled the preservation of the main part of Soviet industry and ensured the continuation of war production in the Ural-Volga region. The author notes the transition to a military-oriented socialist economy during wartime, characterized by state-organized recruitment of manpower and the implementation of the war economy plan, and the rapid development of heavy industry and the armaments industry.

The article is devoted to several key issues including comparison of the advantages and drawbacks of the planned economy as the war economy of the Russian Empire in WWI and the role of women and youth in solving the labour shortages as well as the issues of 'expanded reproduction' in the East. The article includes a graphic description of the catastrophic destruction of the occupied lands and an epic description of the restoration of these areas during the war. The authors come to a general conclusion that the Soviet country's war economy — based on the industrial base created during the Five-Year Plans — possessed qualitative and quantitative features that ensured superiority in military technology, and which ultimately led to Germany's economic and military downfall.
